meta.title: Partial Pre-rendering (PPR): The Future of Rendering
meta.excerpt: Combine static shells with dynamic content for optimal user experience. Learn how PPR gives you the best of both SSG and SSR.

props.content: PPR is a Next.js rendering strategy that pre-renders a static shell of your page at build time, with dynamic placeholders that are filled in at request time. This gives you instant static loading with streaming dynamic content.

PPR represents a paradigm shift in how we think about rendering. Instead of choosing between static OR dynamic for an entire page, PPR lets you have both simultaneously. The static parts load instantly while dynamic parts stream in as they become ready.

The problem PPR solves: traditional approaches force a choice between SSG (fast but can't have dynamic content) or SSR (dynamic but slower initial load). PPR: static shell loads instantly, dynamic parts stream in.
PPR is available in Next.js 14+ and became stable in Next.js 15. Enable it in your next.config.js to use this feature.

props.title: Create Static Shell with Dynamic Holes

props.content: Use Suspense boundaries to mark dynamic content that should stream in:

features.title: Instant Initial Load
features.description: Static shell is served from CDN edge, giving users immediate visual feedback while dynamic content loads.

features.title: Progressive Enhancement
features.description: Users see the page structure immediately, then watch content stream in. Better perceived performance.

features.title: Optimal Core Web Vitals
features.description: Fast LCP from static content, good INP from hydrated interactivity, minimal CLS with proper skeletons.

features.title: SEO Friendly
features.description: Search engines can crawl the static shell immediately. Most important content should be static for best SEO.

steps.title: 1. Design Good Skeletons
steps.description: Skeleton loaders should match the final content's dimensions to prevent layout shift (CLS). Use CSS animations to indicate loading state.

steps.title: 2. Prioritize Critical Content
steps.description: Make your most important content static. Put personalization and user-specific data in Suspense boundaries.

steps.title: 3. Group Related Dynamic Content
steps.description: Use a single Suspense boundary for related dynamic content to reduce the number of streaming chunks.

steps.title: 4. Consider Nested Suspense
steps.description: For complex pages, nest Suspense boundaries to show progressive loading states as data becomes available.

props.content: PPR would be excellent for our electrical services platform in scenarios like:

features.title: Quote form
features.description: Static form shell with dynamic pricing estimates

features.title: Service pages
features.description: Static service info with dynamic availability

features.title: Dashboard
features.description: Static layout with personalized service history

props.content: PPR represents the future of web rendering in Next.js. Use it when you have pages with a mix of static content and dynamic, personalized data. The static shell gives instant loads while Suspense boundaries stream in user-specific content, providing the best user experience and performance metrics.
